expanded_description: Carboxylic acids are organic compounds containing a carboxyl functional group ($-\text{COOH}$) consisting of a carbonyl group ($\text{C}=\text{O}$) and a hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$) bonded to the same carbon atom. This section explores carboxylic acid structure and electronic properties, their distinctive physical properties arising from hydrogen bonding and dimerization, systematic IUPAC and common nomenclature conventions, and their characteristic acidity and reactivity. Carboxylic acids are among the most important functional groups in organic chemistry, biochemistry, and industrial chemistry, found in amino acids, fatty acids, pharmaceuticals, and countless natural products.

core_concept.principle: The Carboxyl Group ($-\text{COOH}$) Combines Carbonyl and Hydroxyl Functionality
core_concept.explanation: Carboxylic acids contain a carboxyl group where a carbonyl carbon ($\text{C}=\text{O}$) is bonded to a hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$). The carbonyl carbon is $sp^2$ hybridized with trigonal planar geometry ($\angle \approx 120^\circ$). The combination of electron-withdrawing carbonyl and electron-donating hydroxyl creates unique electronic properties: resonance stabilization of the conjugate base (carboxylate anion) makes carboxylic acids significantly more acidic than alcohols ($\text{p}K_a \approx 4-5$ vs. $16-18$). The $-\text{COOH}$ group enables extensive hydrogen bonding, including dimerization in nonpolar solvents, which elevates boiling points and affects solubility.

image.alt: Diagram showing carboxylic acid carboxyl group with resonance structures
image.caption: Carboxyl group: carbonyl ($\text{C}=\text{O}$) + hydroxyl ($-\text{OH}$) on same carbon; resonance stabilization of carboxylate anion enhances acidity.

sections.introduction: Carboxylic acids are organic compounds characterized by a carboxyl functional group ($-\text{COOH}$) in which a carbonyl carbon is bonded to both an oxygen (via double bond) and a hydroxyl group (via single bond). The general formula for a simple carboxylic acid is $\text{R}-\text{COOH}$, where $\text{R}$ represents hydrogen (formic acid) or an alkyl/aryl group. Carboxylic acids are among the most important functional groups in chemistry: acetic acid is the main component of vinegar; amino acids are the building blocks of proteins; fatty acids are components of lipids; aspirin and many pharmaceuticals contain carboxylic acid groups. Understanding carboxylic acid structure and properties is essential for organic synthesis, biochemistry, and industrial applications.

key_concept.definition: A carboxylic acid is an organic compound containing a carboxyl group ($-\text{COOH}$) where the carbonyl carbon is $sp^2$ hybridized and bonded to both a carbonyl oxygen ($\text{C}=\text{O}$) and a hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$). The general formula is $\text{R}-\text{COOH}$ for most carboxylic acids.
key_concept.context: Carboxylic acids are distinguished from other carbonyl compounds by the presence of the hydroxyl group on the carbonyl carbon. This structural feature enables resonance stabilization of the conjugate base (carboxylate anion, $\text{R}-\text{COO}^-$), making carboxylic acids significantly more acidic than alcohols. Carboxylic acids are classified by the nature of the attached group (aliphatic, aromatic) and by the number of carboxyl groups (monocarboxylic, dicarboxylic, polycarboxylic).

image.alt: General structure of carboxylic acids showing R-COOH format
image.caption: Carboxylic acid general structure: $\text{R}-\text{COOH}$ where $\text{R}$ = H or alkyl/aryl group; carbonyl carbon is $sp^2$ hybridized with trigonal planar geometry.

principles.statement: The carboxyl group's acidity arises from resonance stabilization of the carboxylate anion ($\text{R}-\text{COO}^-$), where the negative charge is delocalized equally over both oxygen atoms.
principles.application: This resonance stabilization makes carboxylic acids much more acidic than alcohols ($\text{p}K_a \approx 4-5$ vs. $16-18$). The conjugate base (carboxylate) is stabilized by two equivalent resonance structures, lowering the energy of the deprotonated form and favoring acid dissociation. Electron-withdrawing substituents further enhance acidity; electron-donating groups decrease it.
principles.analogy: Think of the negative charge in a carboxylate anion like a weight shared between two people (the two oxygens): each oxygen carries only half the burden, making the arrangement more stable than if one oxygen carried the full charge (as in an alkoxide ion from alcohols).
principles.example: Acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$, $\text{p}K_a = 4.76$) is $10^{11}$ times more acidic than ethanol ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$, $\text{p}K_a = 16$). The acetate anion ($\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-$) has two equivalent resonance structures; the ethoxide anion ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{O}^-$) has no resonance stabilization.

principles.statement: Carboxylic acids form strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds, and in nonpolar solvents or the gas phase, they exist as dimers (two molecules held together by two hydrogen bonds).
principles.application: This dimerization effectively doubles the molecular weight in nonpolar environments, significantly elevating boiling points compared to alcohols of similar molecular weight. In water, carboxylic acids form hydrogen bonds with water molecules, enhancing solubility for small acids. The dimer structure is stabilized by two $\text{O}-\text{H} \cdots \text{O}$ hydrogen bonds in a cyclic arrangement.
principles.analogy: Carboxylic acid dimers are like molecular 'handcuffs': two acid molecules link together through two hydrogen bonds, forming a stable pair. This pairing makes them harder to separate (higher boiling point) than single molecules.
principles.example: Acetic acid ($\text{MW} = 60$, $\text{BP} = 118^\circ\text{C}$) boils much higher than 1-propanol ($\text{MW} = 60$, $\text{BP} = 97^\circ\text{C}$) despite similar molecular weight. The difference is due to carboxylic acid dimerization creating effectively $\text{MW} = 120$ species in the vapor phase.

components.definition: The carboxyl functional group consists of an $sp^2$ hybridized carbonyl carbon bonded to a carbonyl oxygen via a double bond ($\text{C}=\text{O}$), to a hydroxyl oxygen via a single bond ($\text{C}-\text{OH}$), and to one carbon group (or H in formic acid). The carboxyl group is planar with bond angles $\approx 120^\circ$.
components.interpretation: The trigonal planar geometry and conjugated $\pi$-system create a rigid, flat structure around the carboxyl group. In the carboxylate anion ($\text{R}-\text{COO}^-$), both $\text{C}-\text{O}$ bonds are equivalent ($127 \text{ pm}$) due to resonance. The acidic proton is on the hydroxyl oxygen; deprotonation creates the resonance-stabilized carboxylate.

image.alt: Diagram showing carboxylic acid bond angles and lengths
image.caption: Carboxylic acid structure: $sp^2$ carbonyl carbon with $\approx 120^\circ$ bond angles; polar $\text{C}=\text{O}$ and $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bonds enable hydrogen bonding.

sections.introduction: Carboxylic acids can be classified in multiple ways: by the nature of the attached group (aliphatic, aromatic), by the number of carboxyl groups (monocarboxylic, dicarboxylic, polycarboxylic), and by chain length and substitution. These classifications predict physical properties, acidity, and appropriate nomenclature conventions.

classifications.type: By Attached Group Type: Aliphatic vs. Aromatic
classifications.value: Classified by whether the group attached to carboxyl is alkyl (aliphatic) or aryl (aromatic)
characteristics: Aliphatic acids: $\text{R}$ = alkyl group (e.g., acetic acid)
Aromatic acids: $\text{R}$ = aryl group (e.g., benzoic acid)
Aromatic acids are slightly more acidic due to resonance
Aliphatic acids are generally more soluble in water for small chains
classifications.behavior: Aliphatic acids undergo typical carboxylic acid reactions. Aromatic acids have the carboxyl group conjugated with the aromatic ring, which can affect reactivity in electrophilic aromatic substitution (carboxyl is meta-directing and deactivating). Aromatic acids often have higher melting points due to better crystal packing.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$: methyl group attached to carboxyl. Most common carboxylic acid; main component of vinegar ($4-8\%$ solution). Used in chemical synthesis, food industry, and as solvent. $\text{BP} = 118^\circ\text{C}$, $\text{p}K_a = 4.76$.

image.caption: Acetic acid: simplest aliphatic carboxylic acid with methyl group attached to carboxyl.

examples.explanation: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH}$: phenyl group attached to carboxyl. Carboxyl conjugated with aromatic ring; $\text{p}K_a = 4.20$ (slightly more acidic than acetic acid). Used as food preservative and in organic synthesis. $\text{MP} = 122^\circ\text{C}$.

classifications.value: Classified by the number of $-\text{COOH}$ groups in the molecule
characteristics: Dicarboxylic acids: two $-\text{COOH}$ groups (e.g., oxalic acid, succinic acid)
Polycarboxylic acids: three or more $-\text{COOH}$ groups (e.g., citric acid)
More carboxyl groups = higher acidity and water solubility
classifications.behavior: Each additional carboxyl group increases acidity and water solubility. Dicarboxylic acids can form cyclic anhydrides upon heating. Polycarboxylic acids like citric acid are important in biochemistry (Krebs cycle) and as chelating agents.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$: one carboxyl group. Typical carboxylic acid reactivity. Used in preservatives and plastic production. $\text{BP} = 141^\circ\text{C}$, $\text{p}K_a = 4.87$.

image.caption: Propanoic acid: monocarboxylic acid with three-carbon chain.

examples.explanation: $\text{HOOC}-\text{COOH}$: two carboxyl groups on adjacent carbons. Strongest organic dicarboxylic acid ($\text{p}K_{a1} = 1.27$). Found in spinach and rhubarb; used as cleaning agent and in metal polishing.

$\text{C}_6\text{H}_8\text{O}_7$: three carboxyl groups plus one hydroxyl. Key intermediate in Krebs cycle (cellular respiration). Used as food additive (acidulant, preservative) and in cleaning products. $\text{p}K_{a1} = 3.13$.

classifications.value: Classified by the length and structure of the carbon chain attached to carboxyl
characteristics: Long-chain ($\text{C}_{11}+$): waxy solids, low water solubility (fatty acids)
Branched chains: lower melting points than straight-chain isomers
classifications.behavior: Short-chain acids are liquids with strong, often unpleasant odors (formic acid: pungent; butyric acid: rancid butter). Medium and long-chain acids are fatty acids, components of triglycerides (fats and oils). Saturated fatty acids have higher melting points than unsaturated fatty acids due to better packing.

examples.explanation: $\text{HCOOH}$: simplest carboxylic acid, no alkyl group. Found in ant and bee venom; causes burning sensation. Used in leather tanning and as preservative. $\text{BP} = 101^\circ\text{C}$, $\text{p}K_a = 3.75$.

image.caption: Formic acid: simplest carboxylic acid with hydrogen attached to carboxyl.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)_{16}\text{COOH}$: 18-carbon saturated fatty acid. White waxy solid; component of animal and vegetable fats. Used in soap, cosmetics, and food production. $\text{MP} = 69^\circ\text{C}$.

sections.key_concept: Carboxylic acid classification: by attached group (aliphatic vs. aromatic), by number of $-\text{COOH}$ groups (mono-, di-, polycarboxylic), and by chain length (short, medium, long/fatty acids). Classification predicts acidity, solubility, and physical state.

sections.section_title: IUPAC and Common Nomenclature for Carboxylic Acids
sections.introduction: Carboxylic acids can be named using systematic IUPAC rules or retained common names. The IUPAC system clearly indicates the carbon skeleton structure and carboxyl position, while common names (often derived from natural sources) remain widely used in chemical literature and industry. Understanding both systems is essential for reading chemical literature.

key_concept.definition: IUPAC carboxylic acid names replace the '-e' from the parent alkane with '-oic acid' (e.g., ethanoic acid from ethane). The carboxyl carbon is always position 1, so no position number is needed for simple acids. Common names are often derived from natural sources (formic acid from ants, acetic acid from vinegar).
key_concept.context: For carboxylic acids attached to rings, the suffix '-carboxylic acid' is used (e.g., cyclohexanecarboxylic acid). When the carboxyl group is not the principal functional group, the prefix 'carboxy-' is used. Aromatic acids often retain common names (benzoic acid). Dicarboxylic acids use '-dioic acid' suffix.

image.alt: Diagram showing carboxylic acid naming format with examples
image.caption: Carboxylic acid naming: IUPAC '-oic acid' suffix (ethanoic acid) vs. common names (acetic acid); carboxyl carbon is always position 1.

principles.statement: For IUPAC names, identify the longest carbon chain containing the carboxyl group, replace the '-e' from the parent alkane name with '-oic acid', and number the chain starting from the carboxyl carbon (position 1).
principles.application: This system provides unambiguous names for all carboxylic acids. The carboxyl carbon is automatically position 1, so no locant is needed for simple acids. For substituted acids, number to give substituents the lowest possible positions. For dicarboxylic acids, use '-dioic acid' suffix.
principles.analogy: Think of the carboxyl carbon as the 'head' of the molecule: it always gets position 1, and you count from there. The '-oic acid' suffix is like a badge that says 'carboxylic acid here at the end!'
principles.example: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$: 2 carbons = ethane $\rightarrow$ ethanoic acid. $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$: 3 carbons = propane $\rightarrow$ propanoic acid. $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)\text{COOH}$: 3-carbon chain with methyl = 2-methylpropanoic acid.

principles.statement: Common carboxylic acid names are often derived from natural sources or historical usage: formic acid (Latin 'formica' = ant), acetic acid (Latin 'acetum' = vinegar), butyric acid (Latin 'butyrum' = butter).
principles.application: These names are deeply entrenched in chemical literature and industry. Formic acid ($\text{C}_1$), acetic acid ($\text{C}_2$), propionic acid ($\text{C}_3$), butyric acid ($\text{C}_4$), valeric acid ($\text{C}_5$), caproic acid ($\text{C}_6$). IUPAC names are preferred for systematic work, but common names remain in widespread use.
principles.analogy: Common acid names are like family nicknames with stories: 'formic' reminds us it was first isolated from ants; 'acetic' tells us it's from vinegar. These names are shorter and more memorable than formal IUPAC names.
principles.example: Formic acid (IUPAC: methanoic acid). Acetic acid (IUPAC: ethanoic acid). Propionic acid (IUPAC: propanoic acid). Butyric acid (IUPAC: butanoic acid). Benzoic acid (IUPAC: benzenecarboxylic acid).

image.alt: Chart of common carboxylic acid names
image.caption: Common names: derived from natural sources (formic = ants, acetic = vinegar, butyric = butter).

principles.statement: For carboxylic acids attached to rings, use '-carboxylic acid' suffix for cyclic alkanes. Aromatic acids often use retained common names (benzoic acid) or systematic names (benzenecarboxylic acid).
principles.application: When the $-\text{COOH}$ group is attached directly to a ring, the ring is the parent and '-carboxylic acid' indicates the carboxyl substituent. For benzene derivatives, 'benzoic acid' is the preferred IUPAC name. For dicarboxylic acids on rings, use '-dicarboxylic acid'.
principles.analogy: Ring-attached acids are like 'guests' on the ring 'host': the '-carboxylic acid' suffix tells you the acid is visiting (attached to) the ring rather than being part of the main chain.
principles.example: Cyclohexane + $-\text{COOH}$ = cyclohexanecarboxylic acid. Benzene + $-\text{COOH}$ = benzoic acid (preferred) or benzenecarboxylic acid (systematic). Benzene-1,2-dicarboxylic acid = phthalic acid (common).

image.caption: Cyclic acids: cycloalkanecarboxylic acid format; aromatic acids often use common names (benzoic, phthalic).

worked_examples.title: Naming a Substituted Aliphatic Carboxylic Acid

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the longest chain containing the carboxyl group.
Number from the carboxyl carbon (always position 1).
Identify substituent position with this numbering.
Assemble the name with substituent position.

worked_examples.final_answer: 3-methylbutanoic acid. The carboxyl carbon is automatically position 1; methyl substituent is at position 3. Common name: isovaleric acid.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Carboxyl carbon is always position 1 in IUPAC naming; no locant needed for the $-\text{COOH}$ group itself.

worked_examples.title: Naming an Aromatic Carboxylic Acid with Substituents
worked_examples.problem: Name: benzene ring with $-\text{COOH}$ at position 1 and $-\text{Cl}$ at position 3.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify parent: benzene with carboxyl = benzoic acid.
Number the ring starting at carboxyl carbon (position 1), proceeding to give substituent lowest number.
Assemble name with substituent position.

worked_examples.final_answer: 3-chlorobenzoic acid. The carboxyl group automatically defines position 1 in aromatic acids; chloro is at position 3 (meta to carboxyl). Common name: m-chlorobenzoic acid.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Aromatic carboxylic acids: carboxyl defines position 1 automatically; number ring to give substituents lowest positions.

worked_examples.title: Naming a Dicarboxylic Acid

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the longest chain containing both carboxyl groups.
Both ends are carboxyl groups; no need to specify positions (always 1 and 4).
Use '-dioic acid' suffix for two carboxyl groups.
Assemble complete name.

worked_examples.final_answer: butanedioic acid. Common name: succinic acid. Both carboxyl positions are implicit (1 and 4); '-dioic acid' indicates two carboxyl groups.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Dicarboxylic acids: '-dioic acid' suffix; carboxyl positions at chain ends are implicit.

sections.introduction: Carboxylic acid physical properties are governed by extensive hydrogen bonding, dimerization, and molecular weight. Understanding these trends enables prediction of boiling points, melting points, water solubility, and appropriate handling procedures for different carboxylic acids.

categories.description: Carboxylic acid boiling points are significantly elevated compared to alcohols and other compounds of similar molecular weight due to dimerization and strong hydrogen bonding
categories.detailed_mechanism: Carboxylic acids form dimers (two molecules held by two hydrogen bonds) in nonpolar solvents and the gas phase, effectively doubling molecular weight. This dimerization, combined with strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding, elevates boiling points well above alcohols of similar molecular weight. Boiling points increase with molecular weight due to increased dispersion forces.

examples.explanation: Acetic acid ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O}_2$, $\text{MW} = 60$, $\text{BP} = 118^\circ\text{C}$) vs. 1-propanol ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}$, $\text{MW} = 60$, $\text{BP} = 97^\circ\text{C}$) vs. butane ($\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$, $\text{MW} = 58$, $\text{BP} = -0.5^\circ\text{C}$). Acid BP is highest due to dimerization.

image.alt: Graph comparing carboxylic acid, alcohol, and alkane boiling points
image.caption: Carboxylic acid boiling points are highest due to dimerization and strong H-bonding.

examples.explanation: Two carboxylic acid molecules form a cyclic dimer through two $\text{O}-\text{H} \cdots \text{O}$ hydrogen bonds. This structure is stable in nonpolar solvents and the gas phase, effectively doubling molecular weight.

Formic acid ($\text{C}_1$, $\text{BP} = 101^\circ\text{C}$) < Acetic acid ($\text{C}_2$, $\text{BP} = 118^\circ\text{C}$) < Propanoic acid ($\text{C}_3$, $\text{BP} = 141^\circ\text{C}$) < Butanoic acid ($\text{C}_4$, $\text{BP} = 164^\circ\text{C}$). Each $\text{CH}_2$ adds $\approx 20-25^\circ\text{C}$.

categories.description: Small carboxylic acids are highly water-soluble due to H-bonding with water; solubility decreases with increasing hydrocarbon character
categories.detailed_mechanism: The carboxyl group can both donate and accept hydrogen bonds with water molecules, providing excellent water solubility for small acids. However, the hydrophobic alkyl/aryl groups oppose solvation. Rule of thumb: carboxylic acids with $\leq 4$ carbons are miscible or highly soluble; larger acids show limited solubility.

examples.explanation: Formic acid ($\text{C}_1$): miscible. Acetic acid ($\text{C}_2$): miscible. Propanoic acid ($\text{C}_3$): miscible. Butanoic acid ($\text{C}_4$): miscible. Pentanoic acid ($\text{C}_5$): $4.97 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$. Hexanoic acid ($\text{C}_6$): $1.0 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$. Higher acids: essentially insoluble.

image.alt: Graph of carboxylic acid water solubility vs. chain length
image.caption: Water solubility decreases as hydrocarbon chain length increases.

examples.explanation: Benzoic acid: $0.34 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$ at $25^\circ\text{C}$ (slightly soluble). Solubility increases significantly in hot water ($5.6 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$ at $95^\circ\text{C}$), enabling recrystallization purification. The large hydrophobic aromatic ring limits water solubility despite polar carboxyl.
examples.equation: $\text{Aromatic acids: low water solubility at room temperature}$

examples.explanation: Dicarboxylic acids are more water-soluble than monocarboxylic acids of similar chain length due to two polar carboxyl groups. Oxalic acid ($\text{C}_2$): $10 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$. Succinic acid ($\text{C}_4$): $7 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$. Adipic acid ($\text{C}_6$): $1.5 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$.

categories.description: Carboxylic acid melting points show alternating patterns based on chain length parity (even vs. odd number of carbons) due to crystal packing efficiency
categories.detailed_mechanism: Saturated fatty acids with even-numbered carbon chains pack more efficiently in crystal lattices than odd-numbered chains, resulting in higher melting points. This 'alternation effect' is pronounced in long-chain fatty acids. Aromatic acids generally have higher melting points than aliphatic acids due to better crystal packing from planar aromatic rings.

examples.explanation: Propanoic acid ($\text{C}_3$, $\text{MP} = -21^\circ\text{C}$) < Butanoic acid ($\text{C}_4$, $\text{MP} = -5^\circ\text{C}$) < Pentanoic acid ($\text{C}_5$, $\text{MP} = -34^\circ\text{C}$) < Hexanoic acid ($\text{C}_6$, $\text{MP} = -3^\circ\text{C}$). Even-numbered acids have higher MPs due to better packing.

image.alt: Graph showing even-odd alternation in carboxylic acid melting points
image.caption: Even-numbered fatty acids have higher melting points than odd-numbered due to better crystal packing.

examples.explanation: Benzoic acid: $\text{MP} = 122^\circ\text{C}$ (high for molecular weight due to aromatic stacking). Phthalic acid (1,2-benzenedicarboxylic): $\text{MP} = 207^\circ\text{C}$. Isophthalic acid (1,3-): $\text{MP} = 348^\circ\text{C}$. Terephthalic acid (1,4-): $\text{MP} = 300^\circ\text{C}$ (sublimes). Position of carboxyls affects packing.
examples.equation: $\text{Aromatic acids: high MPs due to planar stacking}$

categories.description: Carboxylic acid acidity ($\text{p}K_a$) varies with substituent effects: electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity; electron-donating groups decrease acidity
categories.detailed_mechanism: Substituents affect acidity through inductive and resonance effects. Electron-withdrawing groups (halogens, nitro, carbonyl) stabilize the carboxylate anion, increasing acidity (lower $\text{p}K_a$). Electron-donating groups (alkyl) destabilize the anion, decreasing acidity (higher $\text{p}K_a$). Effect decreases with distance from carboxyl group.

examples.explanation: Acetic acid ($\text{p}K_a = 4.76$). Chloroacetic acid ($\text{ClCH}_2\text{COOH}$, $\text{p}K_a = 2.87$): Cl withdraws electrons, increasing acidity. Dichloroacetic acid ($\text{p}K_a = 1.29$). Trichloroacetic acid ($\text{p}K_a = 0.66$): more Cl = more acidic.

image.alt: Chart showing substituent effects on carboxylic acid acidity
image.caption: Electron-withdrawing substituents increase acidity; effect decreases with distance.

examples.explanation: Chloroacetic acid ($\alpha$-Cl, $\text{p}K_a = 2.87$) > 3-chloropropanoic acid ($\beta$-Cl, $\text{p}K_a = 4.0$) > 4-chlorobutanoic acid ($\gamma$-Cl, $\text{p}K_a = 4.5$). Inductive effect diminishes with distance from carboxyl.

Benzoic acid ($\text{p}K_a = 4.20$) vs. acetic acid ($\text{p}K_a = 4.76$): aromatic ring slightly withdraws electrons, making benzoic acid more acidic. p-Nitrobenzoic acid ($\text{p}K_a = 3.44$): nitro group further increases acidity.
examples.equation: $\text{Aromatic acids: slightly more acidic than aliphatic}$

sections.key_concept: Carboxylic acid physical properties: highest BPs among similar MW compounds (dimerization); water solubility decreases with chain length ($\leq \text{C}_4$ miscible); melting points show even-odd alternation; acidity ($\text{p}K_a \approx 4-5$) enhanced by electron-withdrawing substituents.

sections.introduction: Carboxylic acids undergo characteristic reactions at the carboxyl group, including acid-base reactions, nucleophilic acyl substitution, reduction, and decarboxylation. Understanding carboxylic acid reactivity patterns is essential for organic synthesis and biochemical transformations.

principles.statement: Carboxylic acids react with bases (hydroxides, carbonates, bicarbonates) to form carboxylate salts and water/CO$_2$, a reaction used to distinguish carboxylic acids from phenols and other weak acids.
principles.application: This is the most characteristic reaction of carboxylic acids. Carboxylate salts are water-soluble even when the parent acid is not, enabling separation and purification. Reaction with $\text{NaHCO}_3$ produces $\text{CO}_2$ gas (effervescence), a diagnostic test for carboxylic acids.
principles.analogy: Think of carboxylic acids as 'proton donors' in a relay race: they pass their acidic proton to a base, becoming carboxylate anions. The carboxylate is like the runner after passing the baton—still important but in a different form (salt).
principles.example: Acetic acid + $\text{NaOH} \rightarrow$ sodium acetate + $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Acetic acid + $\text{NaHCO}_3 \rightarrow$ sodium acetate + $\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$ (effervescence). Benzoic acid (insoluble) + $\text{NaOH} \rightarrow$ sodium benzoate (soluble).

principles.statement: Carboxylic acids undergo nucleophilic acyl substitution reactions to form carboxylic acid derivatives: acid chlorides, anhydrides, esters, and amides.
principles.application: This is the most important synthetic transformation of carboxylic acids. The $-\text{OH}$ group is replaced by another nucleophile ($-\text{Cl}$, $-\text{OCOR}$, $-\text{OR}'$, $-\text{NH}_2$). Direct substitution is slow; acids are often activated (converted to acid chlorides) for efficient substitution. These reactions form the basis of peptide bond formation in biochemistry.
principles.analogy: Think of nucleophilic acyl substitution like replacing a tenant in an apartment: the carboxyl carbon is the apartment, the $-\text{OH}$ is the current tenant, and the nucleophile is the new tenant. The landlord (carbonyl) facilitates the exchange through a temporary 'intermediate' state.
principles.example: Acetic acid + $\text{SOCl}_2 \rightarrow$ acetyl chloride (acid chloride). Acetic acid + ethanol + acid catalyst $\rightarrow$ ethyl acetate (ester). Acetic acid + ammonia + heat $\rightarrow$ acetamide (amide). These are all acyl substitution products.

principles.statement: Carboxylic acids react with alcohols in the presence of acid catalyst to form esters and water; this equilibrium reaction is called Fischer esterification.
principles.application: This is the most common method for preparing esters from carboxylic acids. The reaction is reversible; excess alcohol or removal of water drives equilibrium toward ester. Acid catalyst (typically $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ or $\text{HCl}$) protonates carbonyl, making it more electrophilic. Used extensively in fragrance and flavor synthesis.
principles.analogy: Fischer esterification is like a molecular 'marriage': the carboxylic acid and alcohol join together, releasing water as the 'wedding fee'. The acid catalyst is the 'matchmaker' that brings them together more efficiently.
principles.example: Acetic acid + ethanol $\xrightarrow{\text{H}^+}$ ethyl acetate + $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Butanoic acid + methanol $\xrightarrow{\text{H}^+}$ methyl butanoate (pineapple odor) + $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Salicylic acid + methanol $\xrightarrow{\text{H}^+}$ methyl salicylate (wintergreen oil).

principles.statement: Carboxylic acids are reduced to primary alcohols by strong reducing agents ($\text{LiAlH}_4$); milder agents like $\text{NaBH}_4$ do not reduce carboxylic acids.
principles.application: This transformation converts the carboxyl group ($-\text{COOH}$) to a primary alcohol ($-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$). $\text{LiAlH}_4$ is required because carboxylic acids are less electrophilic than aldehydes/ketones. The reaction proceeds via an aldehyde intermediate that is immediately reduced. Used in synthesis when primary alcohols are needed from acids.
principles.analogy: Reduction is like 'downgrading' the oxidation state: you add hydrogen to the carbonyl carbon, converting the highly oxidized carboxyl group to a less oxidized alcohol. It's like removing oxygen 'weight' from the carbon.
principles.example: Acetic acid + $\text{LiAlH}_4 \rightarrow$ ethanol. Benzoic acid + $\text{LiAlH}_4 \rightarrow$ benzyl alcohol. Stearic acid + $\text{LiAlH}_4 \rightarrow$ 1-octadecanol (fatty alcohol for cosmetics).

principles.statement: Carboxylic acids can lose $\text{CO}_2$ (decarboxylate) under specific conditions, particularly $\beta$-keto acids and malonic acid derivatives which decarboxylate readily upon heating.
principles.application: Decarboxylation shortens the carbon chain by one carbon. $\beta$-Keto acids decarboxylate spontaneously upon mild heating due to a favorable six-membered transition state. This reaction is important in biochemistry (Krebs cycle) and organic synthesis (malonic ester synthesis).
principles.analogy: Decarboxylation is like the carboxyl group 'jumping off' the molecule as $\text{CO}_2$ gas. It's easiest when there's a 'helper' group (like a ketone) at the $\beta$-position that stabilizes the transition state.
principles.example: Acetoacetic acid ($\beta$-keto acid) + heat $\rightarrow$ acetone + $\text{CO}_2\uparrow$. Malonic acid + heat $\rightarrow$ acetic acid + $\text{CO}_2\uparrow$. In biochemistry: pyruvate decarboxylation in cellular respiration.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Esterification Product
worked_examples.problem: Predict the product of reaction: propanoic acid + ethanol + $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ catalyst, heat.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the reaction type: carboxylic acid + alcohol + acid catalyst = Fischer esterification.
Acid catalyst protonates carbonyl oxygen, making carbonyl carbon more electrophilic.
Ethanol attacks electrophilic carbonyl carbon, forming tetrahedral intermediate.
Water is eliminated; ester is formed.

worked_examples.final_answer: Product: ethyl propanoate ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOCH}_2\text{CH}_3$). Fischer esterification converts carboxylic acid + alcohol to ester + water. The ester has a fruity odor.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Fischer esterification: acid-catalyzed reaction of carboxylic acid with alcohol produces ester; water is byproduct; reaction is reversible.

worked_examples.title: Acid-Base Reaction: Distinguishing Carboxylic Acid from Phenol
worked_examples.problem: Explain how $\text{NaHCO}_3$ distinguishes benzoic acid from phenol.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the compounds: benzoic acid ($\text{p}K_a \approx 4.2$) is a carboxylic acid; phenol ($\text{p}K_a \approx 10$) is a weaker acid.
$\text{NaHCO}_3$ is a weak base ($\text{p}K_a$ of $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \approx 6.4$); it deprotonates acids stronger than carbonic acid.
Benzoic acid ($\text{p}K_a = 4.2$) is stronger than carbonic acid; phenol ($\text{p}K_a = 10$) is weaker.
Benzoic acid reaction produces $\text{CO}_2$ gas (effervescence); phenol shows no reaction.

worked_examples.final_answer: Benzoic acid reacts with $\text{NaHCO}_3$ to produce $\text{CO}_2$ gas (effervescence); phenol does not react. This distinguishes carboxylic acids ($\text{p}K_a \approx 4-5$) from phenols ($\text{p}K_a \approx 10$) based on acidity.
worked_examples.concept_applied: $\text{NaHCO}_3$ test: carboxylic acids (stronger than $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$) produce $\text{CO}_2$; phenols (weaker) do not. Diagnostic test for carboxylic acids.

worked_examples.title: Reduction of Carboxylic Acid
worked_examples.problem: Predict the product: butanoic acid + $\text{LiAlH}_4$, then $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$ workup.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the reaction type: $\text{LiAlH}_4$ is a strong reducing agent; this is carboxylic acid reduction.
$\text{LiAlH}_4$ delivers hydride to carbonyl carbon, forming aldehyde intermediate.
Aldehyde is immediately reduced further to primary alcohol (cannot be isolated).
Acidic workup protonates alkoxide to form alcohol.

worked_examples.final_answer: Product: butan-1-ol ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$). Carboxylic acid reduction with $\text{LiAlH}_4$ produces primary alcohols. $\text{NaBH}_4$ is not strong enough to reduce carboxylic acids.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Carboxylic acid reduction: $\text{LiAlH}_4$ reduces $-\text{COOH}$ to $-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$ (primary alcohol); $\text{NaBH}_4$ does not reduce carboxylic acids.

sections.section_title: Common Carboxylic Acids and Their Applications
sections.introduction: Several simple and naturally occurring carboxylic acids have major industrial, pharmaceutical, and biological importance. Understanding these common acids provides context for why carboxylic acid chemistry is practically significant in manufacturing, food, biochemistry, and synthesis.

components.function: Leather tanning; textile dyeing; preservative; ant/bee venom component
components.key_feature: Simplest carboxylic acid; $\text{BP} = 101^\circ\text{C}$; pungent odor; found in ant venom (causes burning sensation); used in agriculture and rubber production

components.function: Vinegar ($4-8\%$ solution); chemical feedstock (vinyl acetate, acetic anhydride); solvent; food preservative
components.key_feature: Most common carboxylic acid; $\text{BP} = 118^\circ\text{C}$; miscible with water; produced by fermentation or methanol carbonylation; glacial acetic acid = pure ($>99\%$)

components.function: Food preservative (sodium benzoate); pharmaceutical intermediate; precursor to plasticizers and dyes
components.key_feature: Aromatic carboxylic acid; $\text{MP} = 122^\circ\text{C}$; slightly soluble in cold water, soluble in hot water (recrystallization); $\text{p}K_a = 4.20$; occurs naturally in berries

components.function: Cleaning agent (rust removal); metal polishing; bleaching; found in spinach and rhubarb
components.key_feature: Strongest organic dicarboxylic acid ($\text{p}K_{a1} = 1.27$); toxic in high doses; forms insoluble calcium oxalate (kidney stones); $\text{MP} = 189^\circ\text{C}$ (sublimes)

components.function: Food additive (acidulant, preservative, flavoring); cleaning products; pharmaceutical buffer; Krebs cycle intermediate
components.key_feature: Tricarboxylic acid; found in citrus fruits; $\text{p}K_{a1} = 3.13$; chelates metal ions; produced industrially by fungal fermentation; $\text{MP} = 153^\circ\text{C}$

components.function: Components of triglycerides (fats and oils); soap production; cosmetics; nutrition
components.key_feature: Long-chain carboxylic acids; saturated (stearic) vs. unsaturated (oleic, linoleic); essential fatty acids for health; form micelles and bilayers in water

components.function: Protein building blocks; neurotransmitters; metabolic intermediates; nutrition
components.key_feature: Contain both amino and carboxyl groups; zwitterionic at physiological pH; 20 standard amino acids in proteins; essential vs. non-essential

sections.introduction: Carboxylic acids produce characteristic signals in infrared (IR) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, enabling rapid identification without chemical tests. Learning these spectroscopic 'fingerprints' is essential for structure determination in organic chemistry.

categories.description: Carboxylic acids show two characteristic IR absorptions: carbonyl stretch and broad O-H stretch
categories.detailed_mechanism: The $\text{C}=\text{O}$ stretch appears at $1700-1725 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ for carboxylic acids. The $\text{O}-\text{H}$ stretch is very broad ($2500-3300 \text{ cm}^{-1}$) due to strong hydrogen bonding and dimerization, often overlapping the $\text{C}-\text{H}$ region. This combination is diagnostic for carboxylic acids.

examples.explanation: Strong, sharp peak at $1700-1725 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. Aliphatic acids: $\sim 1710 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. Aromatic acids: $\sim 1690 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (conjugation lowers frequency). Dimerization affects exact position.

image.alt: IR spectrum showing carboxylic acid carbonyl stretch region
image.caption: Carboxylic acid $\text{C}=\text{O}$ stretch: strong peak $1700-1725 \text{ cm}^{-1}$.

examples.explanation: Very broad peak at $2500-3300 \text{ cm}^{-1}$, often described as 'hairy' or 'bearded' due to extensive H-bonding. This distinguishes carboxylic acids from alcohols (narrower $\text{O}-\text{H}$ at $3200-3600 \text{ cm}^{-1}$).

categories.description: The carboxylic acid proton is highly deshielded and appears as a distinctive downfield signal
categories.detailed_mechanism: The carboxylic acid proton ($-\text{COOH}$) is strongly deshielded and appears at $\delta 10-13 \text{ ppm}$, one of the most downfield signals in organic NMR. This is highly diagnostic for carboxylic acids. The signal is typically broad due to hydrogen bonding and exchange. $\alpha$-protons appear at $\delta 2.0-2.7 \text{ ppm}$.

examples.explanation: Acid $\text{H}$: $\delta 10-13 \text{ ppm}$ (broad singlet). This is the most distinctive NMR feature of carboxylic acids. Aldehyde protons appear at $\delta 9-10 \text{ ppm}$ (slightly upfield). The broad, very downfield signal is diagnostic.

image.alt: NMR spectrum showing carboxylic acid proton region
image.caption: Carboxylic acid proton: highly deshielded, broad signal at $\delta 10-13 \text{ ppm}$.

examples.explanation: Protons on carbon adjacent to carboxyl ($\alpha$-protons): $\delta 2.0-2.7 \text{ ppm}$. Deshielded by carbonyl electron-withdrawal. Similar to ketones/aldehydes.

The acid proton exchanges with $\text{D}_2\text{O}$, causing the $\delta 10-13 \text{ ppm}$ signal to disappear. This confirms the signal is from an exchangeable proton ($-\text{OH}$, $-\text{NH}$, $-\text{COOH}$).

categories.description: The carboxylic acid carbonyl carbon appears at characteristic downfield position
categories.detailed_mechanism: The carbonyl carbon in carboxylic acids appears at $\delta 170-185 \text{ ppm}$ in $^{13}\text{C}$ NMR, upfield from ketones/aldehydes ($\delta 190-220 \text{ ppm}$) due to resonance donation from the hydroxyl oxygen. This helps distinguish carboxylic acids from other carbonyl compounds.

examples.explanation: Carboxylic acid $\text{C}=\text{O}$: $\delta 170-185 \text{ ppm}$. Aldehyde $\text{C}=\text{O}$: $\delta 190-200 \text{ ppm}$. Ketone $\text{C}=\text{O}$: $\delta 200-220 \text{ ppm}$. Ester $\text{C}=\text{O}$: $\delta 160-180 \text{ ppm}$. This range helps identify carbonyl type.

sections.key_concept: IR: $\text{C}=\text{O}$ at $1700-1725 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ + very broad $\text{O}-\text{H}$ at $2500-3300 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (diagnostic). $^1\text{H}$ NMR: acid $\text{H}$ at $\delta 10-13 \text{ ppm}$ (broad, exchangeable). $^{13}\text{C}$ NMR: carbonyl $\text{C}$ at $\delta 170-185 \text{ ppm}$.

sections.section_title: Common Mistakes in Carboxylic Acid Chemistry
sections.introduction: Carboxylic acid chemistry presents unique challenges in identification, naming, and reactivity prediction. Understanding common pitfalls helps ensure accurate communication of carboxylic acid structures and realistic expectations of their behavior in chemical contexts.

categories.description: Adding position number for carboxyl carbon in IUPAC naming
categories.detailed_mechanism: The carboxyl carbon is always position 1 in IUPAC naming, so specifying '1-' is redundant and incorrect. Students sometimes write 'butan-1-oic acid' instead of 'butanoic acid'.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: butan-1-oic acid (redundant position). CORRECT: butanoic acid (carboxyl carbon is automatically position 1). Position numbers are only needed for substituents on the chain.

categories.type: Mistake: Confusing Carboxylic Acids with Other Carbonyls
categories.description: Misidentifying carboxylic acids as aldehydes, ketones, or esters based on carbonyl presence alone
categories.detailed_mechanism: Students sometimes focus only on the $\text{C}=\text{O}$ and miss the $-\text{OH}$ component. Carboxylic acids have both $\text{C}=\text{O}$ and $-\text{OH}$ on the same carbon; aldehydes/ketones have only $\text{C}=\text{O}$; esters have $\text{C}=\text{O}$ and $-\text{OR}$. IR and NMR distinguish these.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: Calling $\text{CH}_3\text{COOCH}_3$ a carboxylic acid. CORRECT: It is methyl acetate (an ester) because the oxygen is bonded to carbon (methoxy), not hydrogen. Acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$) has $-\text{OH}$ on carbonyl.

categories.type: Mistake: Assuming NaBH4 Reduces Carboxylic Acids
categories.description: Expecting sodium borohydride to reduce carboxylic acids to alcohols
categories.detailed_mechanism: $\text{NaBH}_4$ is not strong enough to reduce carboxylic acids; only $\text{LiAlH}_4$ or other strong reducing agents work. Students may plan syntheses using $\text{NaBH}_4$ for acid reduction, leading to failed reactions.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 'Acetic acid + $\text{NaBH}_4 \rightarrow$ ethanol.' CORRECT: Acetic acid + $\text{NaBH}_4 \rightarrow$ NO REACTION. Must use $\text{LiAlH}_4$: acetic acid + $\text{LiAlH}_4 \rightarrow$ ethanol.

categories.description: Confusing carboxylic acid O-H with alcohol O-H in IR spectroscopy
categories.detailed_mechanism: Carboxylic acid $\text{O}-\text{H}$ stretch is very broad ($2500-3300 \text{ cm}^{-1}$) due to dimerization; alcohol $\text{O}-\text{H}$ is narrower ($3200-3600 \text{ cm}^{-1}$). Students may misidentify based on $\text{O}-\text{H}$ presence alone without noting the breadth.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: Identifying a compound as an alcohol based on $\text{O}-\text{H}$ stretch at $3000 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. CORRECT: Check breadth—very broad $2500-3300 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ + $\text{C}=\text{O}$ at $1710 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ = carboxylic acid; narrower $3200-3600 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ without $\text{C}=\text{O}$ = alcohol.

categories.description: Using '-oic acid' instead of '-dioic acid' for dicarboxylic acids
categories.detailed_mechanism: Dicarboxylic acids require the '-dioic acid' suffix to indicate two carboxyl groups. Students sometimes name them as substituted monocarboxylic acids, which is incorrect.

examples.explanation: Structure: $\text{HOOC}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{COOH}$. INCORRECT: 4-carboxybutanoic acid. CORRECT: butanedioic acid (succinic acid). Both carboxyls are part of the parent chain; use '-dioic acid'.

sections.section_title: Carboxylic Acid Nomenclature and Properties Summary Checklist
sections.introduction: A quick-reference summary consolidates carboxylic acid naming rules and property trends into an actionable checklist for identifying, naming, and predicting behavior of carboxylic acid compounds.

components.composition: Look for $-\text{COOH}$ with both $\text{C}=\text{O}$ and $-\text{OH}$ on same carbon
components.function: Distinguishes carboxylic acids from other carbonyls

components.composition: Longest chain with $-\text{COOH}$; replace '-e' with '-oic acid'; carboxyl = position 1
components.key_feature: No position number needed for $-\text{COOH}$ itself; number substituents from carboxyl end

components.composition: Highest BP among similar MW (dimerization); water solubility decreases with chain length
Acidic ($\text{p}K_a \approx 4-5$); nucleophilic acyl substitution; reduction with $\text{LiAlH}_4$
components.function: Informs reaction conditions and synthesis planning
components.key_feature: Forms carboxylate salts with bases; esterification with alcohols; $\text{NaHCO}_3$ test positive

components.composition: IR: $\text{C}=\text{O}$ $1700-1725 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ + broad $\text{O}-\text{H}$ $2500-3300 \text{ cm}^{-1}$; NMR: $\delta 10-13 \text{ ppm}$
components.function: Confirms carboxylic acid identity in unknown compounds
components.key_feature: Very broad $\text{O}-\text{H}$ in IR and $\delta 10-13 \text{ ppm}$ signal in NMR are diagnostic

summary: Carboxylic acids are organic compounds containing a carboxyl group ($-\text{COOH}$) where the carbonyl carbon is $sp^2$ hybridized and bonded to both a carbonyl oxygen ($\text{C}=\text{O}$) and a hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$). Resonance stabilization of the carboxylate anion ($\text{R}-\text{COO}^-$) makes carboxylic acids significantly more acidic than alcohols ($\text{p}K_a \approx 4-5$ vs. $16-18$). Dimerization through two hydrogen bonds elevates boiling points well above alcohols of similar molecular weight. Classification: by attached group (aliphatic vs. aromatic), by number of $-\text{COOH}$ groups (mono-, di-, polycarboxylic), and by chain length (short, medium, long/fatty acids). IUPAC nomenclature: replace '-e' from parent alkane with '-oic acid' (e.g., ethanoic acid); carboxyl carbon is always position 1 (no locant needed); common names derived from natural sources (formic, acetic, benzoic acid). Physical properties: highest BPs among similar MW compounds (dimerization); water solubility decreases with chain length ($\leq \text{C}_4$ miscible); melting points show even-odd alternation in fatty acids. Chemical reactivity: acid-base reactions (form carboxylate salts with bases; $\text{NaHCO}_3$ test produces $\text{CO}_2$), nucleophilic acyl substitution (acid chlorides, anhydrides, esters, amides), Fischer esterification (acid + alcohol $\rightleftharpoons$ ester + water), reduction to primary alcohols ($\text{LiAlH}_4$ required, not $\text{NaBH}_4$), decarboxylation (especially $\beta$-keto acids). Spectroscopic identification: IR shows $\text{C}=\text{O}$ at $1700-1725 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ + very broad $\text{O}-\text{H}$ at $2500-3300 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (diagnostic); $^1\text{H}$ NMR shows acid proton at $\delta 10-13 \text{ ppm}$ (broad, exchangeable with $\text{D}_2\text{O}$); $^{13}\text{C}$ NMR shows carbonyl carbon at $\delta 170-185 \text{ ppm}$. Common carboxylic acids include formic acid (ant venom), acetic acid (vinegar), benzoic acid (preservative), oxalic acid (cleaning), citric acid (Krebs cycle), fatty acids (lipids), and amino acids (proteins). Critical considerations: carboxyl carbon is always position 1 in naming; $\text{LiAlH}_4$ required for reduction (not $\text{NaBH}_4$); spectroscopic fingerprints (broad $\text{O}-\text{H}$ in IR, $\delta 10-13 \text{ ppm}$ in NMR) distinguish from other carbonyls; electron-withdrawing substituents increase acidity.
